senses.gloss: having partitive effect: of, some of, parts of, one of

examples.english: Is there not a right-minded man among you?
a ring of gold, a gold ring

senses.gloss: in relation to, with respect to

examples.english: He differentiated/discerned between the good and the bad

examples.text: مَالِي أَكْثَرُ مِنْ مَالِكَ / أَنَا أَكْثَرُ مِنْكَ مَالاً
examples.english: I have more money than you.

senses.gloss: who, the one who, he who, those who, everyone who

examples.text:
فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْرًا يَرَهُ
وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ
examples.english:
So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it,
 And whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it.

senses.gloss: to be kind, kindly, benign, gracious, benevolent (عَلَى (ʕalā))
to show, to grant, to confer (عَلَى (ʕalā))

examples.text: إن المسلمين يعرفون المهارات القيادية المختلفة التي مَنَّ بها الله على نبيّه الكريم.
examples.english: Muslims know the different leadership skills that God has bestowed upon His noble Prophet.

senses.gloss: to decrease, to diminish, to reduce

examples.text: وَتِلْكَ نِعْمَةٌ تَمُنُّهَا عَلَيَّ أَنْ عَبَّدْتَ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ
examples.english: And this is the past favour wherewith thou reproachest me: that thou hast enslaved the Children of Israel.

examples.text: يَا بَنِي إِسْرَاءِيلَ قَدْ أَنْجَيْنٰكُمْ مِنْ عَدُوِّكُمْ وَوٰعَدْنٰكُمْ جَانِبَ ٱلطُّورِ ٱلْأَيْمَنَ وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَنَّ وَٱلسَّلْوَى
examples.english: O sons of Israel, We did deliver you from your enemy and We did covenant with you on the right side of the Mount [Sinai] and We did send down unto you the manna and the quails.

examples.text: وَأَكَلَ بَنُو إِسْرَاءِيلَ ٱلْمَنَّ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً حَتَّى جَاءُوا إِلَى أَرْضٍ عَامِرَةٍ.
examples.english: And the sons of Israel have eaten the manna [for] forty years, until their coming to the land to be inhabited.

senses.gloss: a sweet liquid substance such as honey, nectar, or manna; honeydew
a historical unit of mass – translatable as mina or maund – varying usually between ca. 1–3 kilograms, however in some cases also going up over 40 kilograms.

etymology.text:
From Proto-West Semitic *min. Cognate with Hebrew מִן (min).

From Proto-Semitic *mann-. Cognates include Aramaic מן (man), Ge'ez መኑ (männu) and Amharic ማን (man).

The noun is modelled on a large scale upon Aramaic מַנָּא / ܡܢܐ (mannā) from the same root.

From Aramaic מָנָא (mānā), from Akkadian 𒈠𒉡𒌑 (manû), whence also Sumerian 𒈠𒈾 (ma-na /⁠mana⁠/) and Hebrew מָנֶה (mānḗ).
